description: I had just gotten out of the car at the second stop on the auto-tour. I was scanning down the canal looking at ducks and coots, and hearing rancorous, repeating songs from Marsh Wren, Common Yellowthroat, and Red-winged Blackbirds, as well as occasional Wilson's Snipe winnows and songs/calls. Underneath all these sounds was something I immediately recognized as the ticking sound of a Yellow Rail. However, I initially was quite skeptical and almost confused by what I was hearing. The steady and repeating ticking sound was coming from deep inside the bulrushes just southwest of the parking lot. The first sounds I heard were probably no more than 5-8 yards from where I was standing.I started making recordings, and paying attention to whether or not the sound moved through the sedges. I noticed that the ticking sound moved slowly westward, shifting about 15 feet per minute in that direction. I felt that this movement ruled out a frog or insect. It also seemed unlikely to be a songbird like a Marsh Wren or Yellowthroat, since it stayed low and never strayed from the steady ticking sound. I figured a songbird engaging perhaps in some unfamiliar calling episode would stop doing that after a time, and erupt into something more familiar and move more quickly. Instead, it sounded like it was coming from a small bird ambling around invisibly through tall growth, like a rail would.I realized that I were correct, I would very likely never see this bird, so I instead focused on just getting as clear and close a recording as possible. In the 45 minutes that I spent at the site, I made about 6-7 recordings ranging from 30 seconds to 5 minutes. The bird did move around the tall growth close to the parking lot, and would stop clicking for short periods before resuming a minute or two later. The cadence of the clicking never changed – it always a 5-click sequence, on endless repeat, but never speeding up or slowing down. The 5 clicks were 2 initial clicks, a higher and lower one, followed by 3 clicks at the same pitch as the higher note. The next phrase would start right after the previous one ended so that the clicks were almost all evenly spaced in time. This is easily seen in a spectrogram.By 730, the bird had started move out of the taller growth and into some shorter grass further south, but still difficult to view. It continued to click even as I left. I did briefly see some shorter grasses swaying near where the sound was emanating about 30 yards away, but strained views through the bins for even a momentary glimpse yielded nothing. This was the extent of my experience of the bird – no visual, but a solid and nearly continuous vocal display unlike anything I'd ever heard at a marsh in Colorado.
